snippets.title: Question-to-chart decision helper (logic only)
snippets.explanation: Encodes the decision framework as explicit rules. Real projects state the question first; this function is a teaching scaffold, not production EDA.

snippets.title: Minimal bar for categorical counts (after decision)
snippets.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

# Decision: "How many per class?" -> bar chart
counts = df["target"].value_counts().sort_index()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4))
ax.bar(counts.index.astype(str), counts.values)
ax.set_xlabel("Class")
ax.set_ylabel("Count")
ax.set_title("Target class balance")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
snippets.explanation: Once the question demands a bar chart, any library can implement it. This snippet shows the smallest Matplotlib path after the chart-type decision is made.

snippets.title: Histogram for one numeric feature (after decision)
snippets.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Decision: "What is the distribution of age?" -> histogram
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.hist(df["age"].dropna(), bins=30, edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("Age (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("Count")
ax.set_title("Age distribution — training set")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
snippets.explanation: Histogram answers univariate numeric shape. Bin count and scaling are tuning details; the chart family choice is the key decision.

snippets.title: Scatter for two numeric features (after decision)
snippets.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Decision: "Do sepal length and width co-vary?" -> scatter
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.scatter(df["sepal_length"], df["sepal_width"], alpha=0.5, s=20)
ax.set_xlabel("Sepal length (cm)")
ax.set_ylabel("Sepal width (cm)")
ax.set_title("Bivariate feature relationship")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
snippets.explanation: Two numeric variables without a natural order on either axis → scatter, not line.
